summary:
The Second Council of Nicaea (787 AD) was the Seventh Ecumenical Council of the Christian Church, called to resolve the [[event:2204|Byzantine Iconoclasm]] controversy that had divided the empire for over sixty years. The council, convened by the empress-regent Irene of Athens and attended by representatives of all the major patriarchates including papal legates from Rome, definitively ruled that the veneration (but not worship) of icons was theologically legitimate and ordered the restoration of images in churches, monasteries, and homes throughout the Byzantine Empire.

The theological distinction the council drew — between the veneration (proskunesis) shown to icons and the worship (latreia) due to God alone — provided the doctrinal framework that still governs Orthodox and Catholic theology on the question of sacred images. The council ruled that veneration of an icon is 'passed on to the prototype': honoring the image of Christ honors Christ himself, not the wood and paint. This theological settlement restored unity between Constantinople and Rome on the icon question, though the respite proved temporary — a Second Iconoclasm broke out from 813–843 AD before the final restoration of icons on the 'Triumph of Orthodoxy' (843 AD), which remains celebrated as a feast day in Orthodox Christianity. The Second Council of Nicaea was the last ecumenical council recognized by both the Eastern Orthodox and Roman Catholic churches, making it a watershed moment before the great schism of 1054 AD permanently divided the two branches of Christianity.
